questions.q: What does IoU (Intersection over Union) measure in object detection?
options: The number of objects detected in an image
The overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding boxes, used to evaluate detection accuracy
The size of the image
The brightness of detected objects
questions.explanation: IoU = Intersection Area / Union Area quantifies bounding box overlap. An IoU ≥ 0.5 is typically used as the threshold for considering a prediction correct. Higher IoU indicates better localization.

questions.q: How do anchor boxes work in object detection frameworks like YOLO or Faster R-CNN?
options: They are fixed boxes placed at image center only
They are predefined box shapes and sizes distributed across the image to predict object locations and dimensions at each position
They represent the final detected objects before post-processing
They are used only for semantic segmentation
questions.explanation: Anchor boxes are pre-defined templates (various aspect ratios/scales) tiled across the image. The model predicts adjustments to these boxes and class probabilities, allowing efficient detection of objects with diverse shapes.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) in object detection?
options: To increase the speed of neural networks
To remove duplicate detections by suppressing overlapping boxes with lower confidence scores
To augment training data
To convert images to grayscale
questions.explanation: NMS eliminates redundant detections of the same object. For overlapping boxes, it keeps the highest-confidence one and removes others with IoU above a threshold, producing clean final detections.

questions.q: How does R-CNN differ from YOLO in approach to object detection?
options: They are identical methods
R-CNN uses region proposals followed by classification; YOLO directly predicts boxes and classes in one pass
YOLO requires two stages while R-CNN requires one
R-CNN only works on small images
questions.explanation: R-CNN generates candidate regions first, then classifies each (slow but accurate). YOLO divides the image into grids and predicts directly (fast, single-stage). Trade-off: R-CNN is more accurate; YOLO is faster.

questions.q: What information does a bounding box in object detection contain?
options: Only the object class label
Coordinates (x, y, width, height or x1, y1, x2, y2), the object class, and confidence score
The exact pixel mask of the object
The object color and texture
questions.explanation: A bounding box is defined by corner coordinates or center position with dimensions, plus the predicted class and confidence. It encloses the detected object region for evaluation and localization.

questions.q: Why is aspect ratio important when defining anchor boxes?
options: It doesn't matter; all anchor boxes should be square
Different objects have different proportions; anchor boxes with varied aspect ratios improve detection of tall, wide, or square objects
It only affects inference speed
It determines the color space used
questions.explanation: Objects vary in shape: people are tall, cars are wide, dogs are more compact. Multiple anchor aspect ratios (e.g., 1:1, 2:1, 1:2) allow the model to match objects of different proportions.

questions.q: What does 'confidence score' represent in object detection predictions?
options: How bright the detected object is
The model's probability that an object exists in the predicted bounding box and how well the box fits that object
The size of the detected object
The background color confidence
questions.explanation: Confidence combines object presence probability and localization quality (IoU with ground truth). High confidence means the model is sure an object exists and the box accurately bounds it.

questions.q: How do Faster R-CNN's Region Proposal Networks (RPN) improve upon standard R-CNN?
options: They require manual bounding box annotation
They generate region proposals directly from convolutional features using anchor boxes, avoiding external tools and improving efficiency
They eliminate the need for training data
They only work with one object per image
questions.explanation: RPN uses the CNN's feature maps to propose regions efficiently, replacing slow external algorithms. This joint learning of proposal and detection accelerates Faster R-CNN over R-CNN.

questions.q: What is a key challenge when detecting very small objects in images?
options: The model architecture must be deeper than 100 layers
Small objects occupy few pixels with limited distinctive features, making them hard to localize and classify accurately
All detection models automatically handle small objects
Small objects are impossible to detect
questions.explanation: Small objects provide limited visual information for feature extraction. Detection frameworks struggle with scale variation; techniques like feature pyramid networks or higher-resolution inputs help address this.

questions.q: In YOLO's design, why is the image divided into a grid during prediction?
options: To reduce image size before processing
Each grid cell predicts objects in its region using anchor boxes; this spatial discretization simplifies the problem and enables parallel prediction across the image
To require more manual annotations
To make training slower
questions.explanation: YOLO divides the image into an S×S grid (e.g., 13×13). Each cell predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities independently, allowing efficient single-pass detection and localization across the entire image.
